title: Spring Core — IoC and Dependency Injection
learningObjectives: Explain the difference between IoC and DI and why Spring uses both
Describe the 6-phase Spring startup lifecycle and what each phase does
Choose the right injection type and bean scope for a given situation
Detect and fix circular dependencies and scope mismatch bugs
Explain proxy wrapping and the self-invocation bypass problem
Answer Spring Core interview questions with production-level precision

sections.title: Why Spring Core IoC and DI matters
sections.content:
When Spring DI is mishandled, three failure patterns appear regularly in production. First, field injection hides missing dependencies until runtime — a misconfigured bean with @Autowired on a private field fails with NullPointerException under load, not at startup where you could catch it during deployment. Second, circular dependencies caught only when field injection is used can work silently for months until a Spring Boot upgrade enables strict mode, then crash on the next deploy with a confusing startup error. Third, prototype-scoped beans injected into singletons via constructor capture a single instance forever, causing per-request state to leak between users — this is a correctness and security bug, not just a performance issue.

Interviewers testing Spring Core are not checking whether you know @Autowired exists. They evaluate whether you understand why the container owns object lifecycle, what the proxy-wrapping step does, and how scope mismatches produce subtle bugs that only appear in concurrent production traffic. The question "why is @Transactional not working on this method?" almost always has an answer rooted in proxy bypass — the method is called via this instead of through the Spring proxy — and that answer requires understanding how the DI container wraps beans at startup.

A strong interview answer about Spring DI follows four steps. First, state the problem the container solves — manual wiring is error-prone, hard to test, and impossible to maintain at scale. Second, explain the mechanism — the ApplicationContext reads BeanDefinitions, instantiates beans in dependency order, injects via constructors, then runs BeanPostProcessors to wrap with proxies. Third, name one real failure mode — scope mismatch, circular dependency, or proxy bypass. Fourth, give the production fix and say how you would verify it worked.

At scale, IoC mistakes compound. A service with 400 beans and 20 using field injection means 20 beans that cannot be unit-tested without a full Spring context — slow integration tests become the only safety net and CI slows down. A misunderstood bean scope means the first concurrent load test surfaces state leakage that was invisible during sequential development. A singleton holding a growing HashMap causes a slow memory leak that only appears after days of production traffic, requires a heap dump to diagnose, and traces back to a single missing cache eviction.

The answer that marks a senior engineer is explaining the startup lifecycle in phases — BeanDefinitions load first, BeanFactoryPostProcessors resolve placeholders, singletons instantiate with dependencies injected, then BeanPostProcessors wrap beans with proxies. That sequence explains why @Conditional decisions are made before any bean exists, why @Transactional only works on externally invoked methods, and why @Lazy defers validation until first use. Knowing the mechanism makes every Spring annotation's behavior predictable instead of magical.

In daily engineering work, IoC and DI problems surface in three recurring ways. During code review, you spot a service with eight @Autowired fields — you know the class is doing too much and the hidden coupling will make the next feature slower. During debugging, a @Transactional method silently commits when it should roll back — you trace the call graph and find it called via this inside the same class. During on-call, you get paged for a memory leak — you pull a heap dump, find a HashMap in a singleton with 400,000 entries, and trace it to a scope mismatch that has been accumulating since the last deploy. Each of these problems has the same root: the engineer who wrote the code did not fully understand who owns object lifetime and how the proxy layer works. Understanding Spring Core at this level is not academic — it directly cuts your debugging time and reduces the number of incidents your team causes.

sections.title: Theory and Internals — Spring Core IoC and DI
sections.content:
### Plain-language overview

**What is Spring doing in simple words?** Spring is a **container**: it creates your objects (beans) and **wires them together** so you do not have to write `new` everywhere. You say what a class **needs** in its constructor (or sometimes setters), and Spring **injects** those dependencies. That pattern is called **dependency injection** (DI).

**Inversion of control (IoC)** means the **framework drives when things happen**. Instead of your code always saying "create this, then that," Spring owns **creation order**, **singleton vs new instance**, and **shutdown**. You still write business logic; Spring handles the plumbing.

**Why it matters (medium level):** Clear constructors make **tests** easy (pass a fake database). **Scopes** answer "how long does this object live?" — usually one shared **singleton** for the whole app, but sometimes you need a **new** instance each time or one per **HTTP request**. A classic interview topic: calling a method with **`this.`** from inside the same class can **skip Spring’s proxy**, so `@Transactional` and other **AOP** features may not run. The sections below explain that mechanism in detail.

**How to read this page:** Start here for a gentle picture, then use the **numbered sections** for lifecycle, injection types, scopes, `@Configuration`, proxies, and circular dependencies.

### Easy English — a bit more detail (medium level)

- Spring acts like a smart factory: it decides **when** to create objects and **how** to plug their dependencies together, so you write less `new` wiring code by hand.
- **Dependency injection** means your class **receives** the collaborators it needs (often through the constructor) instead of creating them inside the class — that makes behaviour easier to swap in tests.
- A **singleton** bean is one shared instance for the whole app; **prototype** can mean a **new** instance when requested — putting short-lived state in a long-lived singleton can leak data between users.
- **AOP** (aspect-oriented programming) often adds features through a **proxy**, a wrapper object in front of your real bean — callers go through the wrapper so extra steps (like opening a database transaction) can run.
- If you call a method with **`this.`** from inside the same class, the call may **skip** the wrapper, so proxy-based features (for example `@Transactional`) might **not** run — that is the famous self-invocation issue.
- A **circular dependency** is when bean A needs B and B needs A to start; constructor injection usually **fails fast at startup**, which is safer than hiding the problem until runtime.

### 1. IoC and DI — precise definitions

**Inversion of Control (IoC)** means the framework calls your code instead of your code calling the framework. In traditional programming you write `new DatabaseRepository()` — you control object creation. IoC inverts that: you declare what you need and the container decides when and how to create it. This is the **Hollywood Principle**: "don't call us, we'll call you."

**Dependency Injection (DI)** is how Spring implements IoC for bean wiring. Your class declares its dependencies in its constructor. Spring reads that declaration and supplies the right objects. DI is one implementation of IoC — they are not synonyms. IoC also appears in `@Scheduled` (container calls your method), `@EventListener` (container delivers events to your handler), and `BeanPostProcessor` (container calls your processor after every bean creation).

### 2. The ApplicationContext — what it owns

The `ApplicationContext` is the IoC container. It owns the full lifecycle of every bean from creation to destruction. When you get a bean from the context, you do not manage its lifecycle — the container does. Key capabilities beyond raw `BeanFactory`: **eager singleton initialization** at startup (wiring errors appear on deploy, not on first request), **event publishing** via `ApplicationEventPublisher`, **i18n** via `MessageSource`, and the **Environment** abstraction for property sources. Spring Boot always creates an `AnnotationConfigServletWebServerApplicationContext` — raw `BeanFactory` is only for framework internals.

### 3. Spring startup lifecycle — 6 phases

Understanding these phases explains every Spring annotation's behavior.

**Phase 1 — Load BeanDefinitions.** Spring reads `@Configuration` classes, `@Component`-scanned classes, and any `@Import`. Each becomes a `BeanDefinition` object in the `BeanDefinitionRegistry`. No instances exist yet — only metadata about how to build them.

**Phase 2 — BeanFactoryPostProcessors.** These run before any bean is instantiated. `PropertySourcesPlaceholderConfigurer` resolves `${...}` values. `@Conditional` annotations are evaluated here — if a condition is false, the BeanDefinition is never registered.

**Phase 3 — Instantiate singletons.** For each singleton, Spring calls the constructor, injects dependencies, calls `@PostConstruct`, then `InitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet()`. Circular constructor dependencies fail here with a clear error.

**Phase 4 — BeanPostProcessors wrap beans.** `AnnotationAwareAspectJAutoProxyCreator` wraps matching beans with CGLIB or JDK dynamic proxies. This is how `@Transactional`, `@Async`, `@Cacheable`, and `@Secured` are applied.

**Phase 5 — ContextRefreshedEvent.** The context is fully ready. `ApplicationRunner` and `CommandLineRunner` beans execute.

**Phase 6 — Shutdown.** `@PreDestroy`, `DisposableBean.destroy()`, custom `destroyMethod` run in reverse registration order.

**Interview angle:** Phase 4 is why self-invocation bypasses AOP — `this.method()` calls the real object, not the proxy created in Phase 4.

### 4. Injection types compared

| Type | Field final? | Test without Spring? | Circular dep | Use when |
|------|-------------|----------------------|--------------|----------|
| Constructor | YES | YES | Fails loud at startup | All required deps |
| Setter | NO | YES via setter | Partial (setter) | Optional deps |
| Field @Autowired | NO | NO (reflection) | Silent failure | Avoid in prod |
| @Value config | YES | YES inline | N/A | Scalar config values |

**Interview angle:** "Field injection hides dependencies, prevents `final` fields, and requires reflection to test. Constructor injection is the Spring team's official recommendation since Spring 4."

### 5. Bean scopes

| Scope | New instance per | Default | Web only |
|-------|-----------------|---------|----------|
| singleton | Container (one shared forever) | YES | NO |
| prototype | Each `getBean()` call | NO | NO |
| request | Each HTTP request | NO | YES |
| session | Each HTTP session | NO | YES |

**Scope mismatch rule:** A shorter-lived bean (prototype) injected into a longer-lived bean (singleton) gets pinned to the singleton's lifetime. Fix: inject `ObjectFactory<T>` or `Provider<T>` and call `getObject()` per use.

**Interview angle:** "Storing a request-scoped value in a singleton field leaks that value across all users — it is a security bug."

### 6. @Configuration and CGLIB proxy

`@Configuration` classes are subclassed by CGLIB at runtime. When `beanA()` calls `beanB()` inside a `@Configuration` class, the CGLIB proxy intercepts the call and returns the existing singleton instead of creating a new instance. Without CGLIB, calling `beanB()` twice would create two separate objects. This is why `@Configuration` classes cannot be `final` — CGLIB cannot subclass final classes. `@SpringBootConfiguration` is a meta-annotation that includes `@Configuration` with the same CGLIB behaviour.

### 7. BeanPostProcessor — how AOP proxies are applied

`BeanPostProcessor` has two methods: `postProcessBeforeInitialization` (before `@PostConstruct`) and `postProcessAfterInitialization` (after). Spring's `AbstractAutoProxyCreator` runs in `postProcessAfterInitialization` and replaces the bean reference with a proxy if any pointcut matches. This is invisible at runtime — `getBean(UserService.class)` returns the proxy, not the real object. Only calls through the proxy trigger advice. Direct `this.method()` calls bypass the proxy entirely.

**Interview angle:** "Every AOP-based annotation — @Transactional, @Async, @Cacheable, @Retryable — relies on this proxy. If a method annotated with @Transactional is called from within the same class using `this`, the proxy is bypassed and the annotation does nothing."

### 8. Circular dependencies — detection and three fixes

A circular dependency exists when Bean A needs Bean B to be created, and Bean B needs Bean A. Spring resolves the dependency graph using topological sort. Constructor injection makes the cycle immediately unresolvable — Spring fails at startup with: `The dependencies of some beans in the application context form a cycle`. This is the correct and safe outcome.

**Fix 1 — Extract a shared bean.** If A and B are circular because both need some shared logic, extract that logic into Bean C. A depends on C, B depends on C. No cycle.

**Fix 2 — @Lazy on one injection point.** Spring injects a proxy placeholder. The real bean initializes on first method call. Breaks the cycle but delays validation — wiring errors appear at runtime under traffic, not at deploy.

**Fix 3 — Setter injection for one dependency.** Spring creates both beans first, then calls the setter. The cycle is resolved but the field cannot be `final`.

### 9. 60-second interview story

"Spring's IoC container reads your `@Component` and `@Bean` declarations and builds a dependency graph. It instantiates beans in the right order — dependencies first — and injects them via constructors. After instantiation, BeanPostProcessors wrap beans with proxies for AOP annotations like @Transactional. The key thing interviewers want to hear is that you understand the proxy step: if you call a @Transactional method from within the same class using `this`, the proxy is bypassed and the transaction never starts. The fix is always to move the call through the proxy — either by extracting to a separate bean or injecting self. That one insight demonstrates you understand Spring at a production level, not just an annotation level."

### 10. Spring's three-level singleton cache — why field injection tolerates circular deps

Spring's **DefaultSingletonBeanRegistry** manages singletons with three internal caches that work together to allow field-injection circular dependencies:

**Level 1 — singletonFactories**: As soon as Spring starts constructing a singleton (after calling the constructor), it registers an ObjectFactory lambda into this map. This lambda can produce an early reference to the partially-constructed bean. This happens *before* any fields are injected.

**Level 2 — earlySingletonObjects**: When another bean needs the not-yet-complete bean, Spring calls the Level 1 factory, gets the early reference, removes it from singletonFactories, and stores it here. The bean is incomplete — fields are not yet set — but the Java reference is stable.

**Level 3 — singletonObjects**: The fully-initialized singleton. When Spring finishes injecting all fields and running @PostConstruct, the bean moves here.

**How a field-injection cycle resolves:** Bean A starts creation → ObjectFactory for A placed in Level 1 → Spring injects Bean B into A's @Autowired field → B needs A → Spring finds A's factory in Level 1 → early A reference moved to Level 2 → B's field receives incomplete A → B finishes → A finishes → both move to Level 3. The early reference in Level 2 is the same Java object identity as the final A in Level 3 — everything is consistent once both are done.

**Why constructor injection cannot use this mechanism:** The constructor call *is* the creation step. For Spring to call new BeanA(BeanB b), BeanB must already be fully created. There is no moment before the constructor where an early reference exists. Hence constructor cycles produce a clean fail-fast: **The dependencies of some beans in the application context form a cycle**.

**Spring Boot 2.6 change:** spring.main.allow-circular-references defaults to false. This disables Level 1/2 resolution. Circular deps that were silently resolving via the three-level cache now fail at startup — forcing teams to fix the design. Re-enable temporarily with spring.main.allow-circular-references=true while migrating legacy code.

**Interview angle:** "The three-level cache explains why @Autowired field injection can sometimes tolerate circular deps while constructor injection always fails loudly. But tolerates does not mean safe — the injected bean is partially initialized when the other bean first uses it, which is a latent correctness hazard when @PostConstruct methods call across the cycle."

### 11. FactoryBean<T> — beans that produce other beans

**FactoryBean<T>** is a Spring SPI interface. A bean implementing it registers the *product* of getObject(), not itself. Key contract:

- **getObject()** — creates or returns the product
- **getObjectType()** — product type used for @Autowired type-matching
- **isSingleton()** — whether to cache the product (default: true)

**Access rules in code:**
- context.getBean("dataSource") returns the product (a DataSource)
- context.getBean("&dataSource") returns the FactoryBean itself (& prefix convention)

**Where you see it every day in production:**
- **Spring Data JPA**: JpaRepositoryFactoryBean creates the UserRepository proxy for every interface extending JpaRepository. The interface you declare is never instantiated — JpaRepositoryFactoryBean.getObject() returns a JDK dynamic proxy.
- **MyBatis-Spring**: SqlSessionFactoryBean builds the SqlSessionFactory from your mapper XML files.
- **Hibernate**: LocalSessionFactoryBean assembles the SessionFactory from entity scanning config.
- **Spring Integration**: MessageChannel factory beans build typed channel implementations.

**When to write your own FactoryBean:** When a third-party client requires multi-step construction and the factory itself needs injected Spring beans. Example: a Kafka AdminClient that needs @Value-injected bootstrap servers, a custom SSL KeyStore bean, and a retry policy bean — all three injected into the FactoryBean's constructor, combined in getObject() to build the client.

**Interview angle:** "When you ask how injecting UserRepository works given it is only an interface, the answer is FactoryBean. Spring Data registers a JpaRepositoryFactoryBean per interface. getObject() returns a JDK dynamic proxy implementing your interface. getBean() gives you the proxy transparently."

### 12. @Import variants — the backbone of auto-configuration

**@Import** has three distinct modes that underpin all of Spring Boot's autoconfiguration:

**Mode 1 — Direct @Configuration import:**
@Import(SecurityConfig.class) registers SecurityConfig as a @Configuration class, equivalent to having it component-scanned. Used in modular apps to assemble explicit configurations without component scanning.

**Mode 2 — ImportSelector:**
Spring calls selectImports(AnnotationMetadata) which returns an array of fully-qualified class names to register as @Configuration beans. @EnableAutoConfiguration uses AutoConfigurationImportSelector which reads:
META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports
Every class name in that file is imported as a @Configuration if its @Conditional conditions pass.

**Mode 3 — ImportBeanDefinitionRegistrar:**
Gets direct programmatic access to BeanDefinitionRegistry. Can register any number of BeanDefinitions of any type. Examples:
- @EnableJpaRepositories scans for Repository interfaces, registers JpaRepositoryFactoryBean per interface
- @EnableCaching registers CacheInterceptor and AnnotationCacheOperationSource
- @ComponentScan delegates to ClassPathBeanDefinitionScanner

**How a Spring Boot Starter works end-to-end:**
1. You add spring-boot-starter-data-redis to pom.xml
2. The JAR's META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports contains RedisAutoConfiguration
3. @EnableAutoConfiguration triggers AutoConfigurationImportSelector at Phase 1
4. RedisAutoConfiguration is imported, but @ConditionalOnClass(RedisConnectionFactory.class) passes (Lettuce is on classpath)
5. @Bean methods in RedisAutoConfiguration register RedisConnectionFactory, RedisTemplate, etc.
6. Your @Autowired RedisTemplate<String, Object> is satisfied automatically

**Interview angle:** "Every @EnableXxx annotation works through @Import. Trace @EnableScheduling: it imports SchedulingConfiguration, which registers ScheduledAnnotationBeanPostProcessor — the BeanPostProcessor that scans for @Scheduled methods and registers them with TaskScheduler."

### 13. @DependsOn, SmartInitializingSingleton, and initialization ordering

**@DependsOn("liquibaseMigration")** forces the named bean to be fully initialized — constructor called, fields injected, @PostConstruct completed — before the annotated bean is created. Spring's dependency ordering algorithm only considers constructor-parameter edges. Side-effect dependencies (database migration must run before repositories start) are invisible unless expressed with @DependsOn.

**Real scenario:** A Flyway migration bean has initMethod = "migrate". Without @DependsOn on the @Configuration that creates JPA repositories, Spring may create the JPA EntityManagerFactory (which triggers schema validation) before Flyway runs. The result is a SchemaValidationException on startup that only occurs in fresh environments with no existing schema.

**@Order controls list injection order, not bean creation order.** @Order(1) on PaymentProcessor A and @Order(2) on B means A appears first in List<PaymentProcessor>. Lower number = higher priority. It does NOT guarantee A is instantiated before B — use @DependsOn for that.

**SmartInitializingSingleton.afterSingletonsInstantiated()** fires after ALL non-lazy singletons are in Level 3 (singletonObjects) — after Phase 4 (BeanPostProcessors applied, all proxies in place) but before Phase 5 (ContextRefreshedEvent, ApplicationRunner.run()). Use it for:
- Cross-bean validation: "Are all required PaymentProcessor beans registered?"
- Pre-warming caches that require multiple fully-initialized beans
- Registering Micrometer metrics whose tag values come from other beans
- Printing a startup summary of active feature flags

**Ordering among BeanFactoryPostProcessors:** BFPP implementations that implement PriorityOrdered run before Ordered, which run before the rest. ConfigurationClassPostProcessor (processes @Configuration and registers all @Bean methods) implements PriorityOrdered at HIGHEST_PRECEDENCE so it always runs first — all other BFPPs depend on @Configuration classes having been processed.

**Interview angle:** "When a NullPointerException occurs inside @PostConstruct calling another bean, the first question is: is that other bean guaranteed to be initialized? If the constructor does not declare it as a parameter, Spring has no ordering obligation. @DependsOn is the explicit declaration of that ordering requirement."

sections.title: Basic — Injection Type, Bean Scope, and Startup Phase Reference Tables
sections.description: Prints three reference tables: injection type comparison, bean scope reference, and Spring startup phases. All output is static strings — easy to trace by eye and memorise for interviews. No Spring on the classpath needed; Spring annotations are shown as comments only.
sections.output:
=== Spring DI — Injection Type Reference ===

Type                 | final? | No-Spring test? | Circular safe?   | Use when
---------------------|--------|-----------------|------------------|--------------------
Constructor          | YES    | YES             | YES (fail loud)  | All required deps
Setter               | NO     | YES (setter)    | Partial (setter) | Optional deps
Field @Autowired     | NO     | NO (reflection) | NO (silent)      | Avoid in prod
@Value config        | YES    | YES (inline)    | N/A              | Scalar config

=== Bean Scope Reference ===

Scope       | New instance per        | Default | Web only
------------|-------------------------|---------|----------
singleton   | Container (shared)      | YES     | NO
prototype   | Each getBean() call     | NO      | NO
request     | Each HTTP request       | NO      | YES
session     | Each HTTP session       | NO      | YES
application | ServletContext lifetime  | NO      | YES

=== Spring Startup Phases ===

Phase | Name                        | Key action
------|-----------------------------|------------------------------------------
1     | Load BeanDefinitions        | Scan @Component, @Bean, process @Import
2     | BeanFactoryPostProcessors   | Resolve ${} placeholders, @Conditional
3     | Instantiate singletons      | Constructor call, inject deps, @PostConstruct
4     | BeanPostProcessors          | Wrap beans with AOP proxies
5     | ContextRefreshedEvent       | Context ready, app is live
6     | Shutdown hooks              | @PreDestroy, DisposableBean.destroy()

Key rule: constructor injection detects circular deps in phase 3 (loud).
          Field injection may hide circular deps until first use (silent).

sections.title: Intermediate — Bean Scope Simulator: Singleton vs Prototype vs ObjectFactory Fix
sections.description: Simulates Spring's singleton and prototype scope behaviour without a framework. Four scenarios show: singleton returns the same instance every call, prototype returns a new instance each call, the scope mismatch bug where a prototype is pinned inside a singleton, and the ObjectFactory fix that gives a fresh prototype on every use.
sections.output:
--- Scenario 1: Singleton scope ---
First call:   userService#instance-1
Second call:  userService#instance-1
Same object:  true

--- Scenario 2: Prototype scope ---
First call:   requestParser#instance-1
Second call:  requestParser#instance-2
Same object:  false

--- Scenario 3: Scope mismatch (prototype pinned in singleton) ---
Singleton:          userService#instance-1
Captured prototype: requestParser#instance-3
Later call (same):  requestParser#instance-3
BUG: prototype is meant to be per-call but is pinned forever

--- Scenario 4: ObjectFactory fix (new instance per call) ---
Factory call 1: requestParser#instance-4
Factory call 2: requestParser#instance-5
Same object:    false
FIX: inject ObjectFactory<T>, call getObject() per use.

sections.title: Advanced — Circular Dependency Detector with DFS
sections.description: Implements the graph algorithm Spring uses internally to detect circular dependencies. Uses depth-first search with a visited set and an in-stack set. Block 1 shows a circular graph that fails; Block 2 shows the refactored graph after extracting a shared bean; Block 3 prints a fix decision table.
sections.output:
=== Block 1: Bean graph with circular dependency ===

Before refactoring:
  OrderService -> [PaymentService]
  PaymentService -> [FraudService]
  FraudService -> [OrderService]
  Result: CYCLE — OrderService -> PaymentService -> FraudService -> OrderService

=== Block 2: After extracting shared FraudChecker ===

After refactoring:
  OrderService -> [PaymentService, FraudChecker]
  PaymentService -> [FraudChecker]
  FraudChecker -> []
  Result: NO CYCLE — context starts successfully.

=== Block 3: Circular dependency fix decision table ===

Situation                          | Fix                           | Permanent?
-----------------------------------|-------------------------------|------------
A needs B, B needs A               | Extract shared bean C         | YES
A needs B, B needs A (urgent fix)  | @Lazy on one injection point  | NO
A needs B for one method only      | Move method to B or new bean  | YES
A and B represent same concept     | Merge into one bean           | YES
A calls B only at runtime          | Inject via ApplicationContext | AVOID

sections.title: Spring IoC — Bean Creation and Proxy Wrapping Flow
sections.description: Shows the six phases of Spring ApplicationContext startup: from classpath scanning through BeanDefinition registration, BeanFactoryPostProcessor resolution, singleton instantiation with constructor injection, BeanPostProcessor AOP proxy wrapping, to the context-ready event.
sections.plantuml:

title Spring ApplicationContext Startup — 6 Phases

participant "Developer\n@Component, @Bean" as Dev
participant "ClassPath Scanner" as Scanner

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Using field injection with @Autowired instead of constructor injection — fields cannot be final, unit tests require ReflectionTestUtils or a full Spring context, and circular dependencies silently resolve via the three-level cache (until Spring Boot 2.6 defaulted allow-circular-references=false); detect by running IntelliJ's Spring inspections or Checkstyle with a rule against @Autowired on fields; fix by moving all dependencies to the constructor.

Storing request-specific state (user ID, locale, trace ID, tenant ID) in a singleton-scoped field — the field is shared across all concurrent HTTP requests; the last writer wins; any thread can read another user's data; this is a GDPR-class security bug; detect by adding a concurrent integration test that fires two requests with different users simultaneously and asserts no cross-contamination; fix by using method parameters, @RequestScope beans, or ThreadLocal cleared in a filter.

Calling a @Transactional, @Async, @Cacheable, or @Retryable method from within the same class using this.method() — Spring's CGLIB or JDK proxy is bypassed; the AOP interceptor never runs; transactions never start; async tasks run synchronously on the caller's thread; detect by adding TransactionSynchronizationManager.isActualTransactionActive() inside the method (it returns false) or by asserting Thread.currentThread().getName() inside @Async; fix by extracting the method to a separate @Component or by injecting self.

Injecting a prototype-scoped bean directly into a singleton constructor — the prototype is instantiated once at singleton creation and pinned in the final field forever; per-call mutable state accumulates across all callers; detect by adding a counter to the prototype and asserting it resets between calls in a test; fix by injecting ObjectFactory<T> or javax.inject.Provider<T> and calling getObject()/get() per invocation, or use @Lookup method injection.

Making a @Configuration class or its @Bean methods final — CGLIB cannot subclass final types; Spring falls back silently to lite mode; @Bean methods called between each other create new instances instead of returning the cached singleton; a DataSource referenced twice creates two connection pools; detect by enabling debug logging (spring.jpa.show-sql and connection pool size alerts); fix by removing all final modifiers from @Configuration classes and @Bean methods.

Running synchronous network calls, loading large files, or blocking on external services inside @PostConstruct — any slow operation delays the entire context startup; any exception prevents the app from starting and causes Kubernetes liveness probes to kill the pod before it becomes Ready; detect by measuring context startup time (management.endpoint.startup.enabled=true, GET /actuator/startup); fix by moving remote calls to ApplicationRunner.run() with retry logic, keeping @PostConstruct for local initialization only.

Using applicationContext.getBean() in business logic or service classes — this is the Service Locator anti-pattern; dependencies are hidden from the constructor (makes the class lie about what it needs), unit testing requires a live context, bypasses lifecycle and AOP proxy management; detect via code review or ArchUnit rule noClasses().should().callMethod(ApplicationContext.class, "getBean"); fix by declaring all dependencies in the constructor and letting Spring inject them.

Treating @Lazy as a permanent solution to circular dependencies — @Lazy injects a CGLIB proxy placeholder that initializes the real bean on the first method call; wiring errors surface under live traffic instead of at deploy; the first caller pays full initialization cost (latency spike); future engineers cannot understand why @Lazy exists without reading git history; @Lazy only masks the design problem, it does not fix it; detect by adding a tech-debt comment and a TODO ticket; fix by extracting the shared dependency into a third bean with no circular relationship to either original bean.

sections.title: Exercise — Circular Dependency Detection and Refactoring
sections.problem:
You are building a small order-processing platform with three Spring beans: `NotificationService` depends on `UserService` to send user-specific alerts, `UserService` depends on `AuditService` to log user actions, and `AuditService` depends on `NotificationService` to send audit alerts. Spring fails to start with 'The dependencies of some beans form a cycle'.

Requirements:
1. Build a dependency graph for the three beans and run a DFS algorithm to detect and print the cycle path.
2. Refactor the graph by extracting a shared `AuditLogger` bean that all three services can depend on without creating a cycle.
3. Re-run the cycle detection on the refactored graph and confirm no cycle exists.
4. Print a summary explaining what the original cycle was and how the extraction fixed it.
hints: Use a visited set and an in-stack set in your DFS — the in-stack set tracks which beans are on the current recursion path, which is what tells you a cycle exists.
The shared extracted bean should have no dependencies of its own — it is a leaf node in the dependency graph.
When printing the cycle path, include the starting bean at both the beginning and end of the list so the cycle is visually obvious.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is Inversion of Control and how is it different from Dependency Injection?
conceptual.answer:
**Inversion of Control (IoC)** is a design principle where the framework calls your code instead of your code calling the framework. In traditional programming you write `new DatabaseRepository()` — you control object creation. With IoC you declare what you need and an external container decides when and how to create and supply it. This is the **Hollywood Principle**: don't call us, we'll call you.

**Dependency Injection (DI)** is one specific way to implement IoC. Your class declares its dependencies in its constructor or setter. The container reads those declarations and injects the right objects at startup. DI is a subset of IoC, not a synonym. IoC also appears in Spring's `@Scheduled` (container calls your method on a timer), `@EventListener` (container delivers events to your handler), and `BeanPostProcessor` (container calls your processor after every bean is created). Every time the framework calls your code rather than the other way around, that is IoC.

followUps.question: Can you implement Dependency Injection without a framework like Spring?
followUps.answer: Absolutely. Manual DI means wiring dependencies yourself in a factory or main method: `var repo = new JdbcUserRepository(dataSource); var service = new OrderService(repo);`. This is pure constructor injection, full IoC, zero framework. The advantage is total transparency — you can read the wiring code like any other Java code and there is no magic. The cost is boilerplate at scale — with 200 beans, manual wiring becomes unmanageable. Spring's container adds value beyond wiring: lifecycle management, scopes, AOP, event publishing, and auto-configuration. Understanding that DI works without a framework makes you a better user of Spring because you know what the container is actually doing on your behalf.

followUps.question: Where else in a Spring application does IoC appear besides @Autowired?
followUps.answer: IoC is everywhere in Spring. `@Scheduled` — your code does not schedule itself; the container calls your method on the configured cron or fixed-rate. `@EventListener` — your code does not poll for events; the container delivers them when published. `@Transactional` — your code does not manage transactions; the container wraps your method via a proxy. `BeanPostProcessor` — your code does not call post-processors; the container calls each one after every bean is created. `ApplicationRunner` and `CommandLineRunner` — your code does not decide when to run; the container calls `run()` after the context is ready. In every case the control flows from the container to your code, not the other way around.

conceptual.question: Why should you prefer constructor injection over field injection in Spring? What breaks if you use field injection?
conceptual.answer:
Constructor injection is the Spring team's official recommendation and the right default for three concrete reasons.

First, **testability**: with constructor injection you write `new OrderService(mockRepo, mockCache)` in a plain JUnit test — no Spring context, no reflection, runs in milliseconds. With field injection you need either `@ExtendWith(SpringExtension.class)` (slow, starts a context) or `ReflectionTestUtils.setField(service, "repo", mockRepo)` (brittle, breaks on renames).

Second, **immutability**: constructor-injected fields can be `final`. The object is fully initialized and sealed the moment it exists. Field injection cannot use `final`, so dependencies could theoretically be replaced after construction.

Third, **hidden coupling**: a constructor is a contract — it documents exactly what a class needs. Field injection scatters dependencies across the class body. A class with 8 `@Autowired` fields is a design smell that constructor injection makes immediately visible; field injection lets that class grow unchecked.

Fourth, **circular dependency detection**: Spring catches constructor cycles at startup and fails loudly. Field injection may silently resolve cycles using early singleton references, then break at runtime when one bean calls the other during its own initialization.

followUps.question: A class has a constructor with nine parameters. Is that a problem with constructor injection?
followUps.answer: No — it is a problem with the class. Nine constructor parameters signal that the class violates the **Single Responsibility Principle** — it is doing too many things. Constructor injection surfaces that design smell immediately, which is exactly the right behavior. The fix is to split the class: group related dependencies into focused collaborators and inject those instead. Field injection would have hidden this problem and let the class grow unchecked for months. When a constructor looks bloated, the correct reaction is to refactor the class design, not switch to field injection to hide the pain.

followUps.question: Is there any situation where field injection is acceptable?
followUps.answer: The one widely accepted exception is `@SpringBootTest` test classes where you are deliberately starting a full application context and injecting the live beans into your test. Since the test itself is not a Spring bean under test, the DI-for-testability argument does not apply. Some teams also accept `@Autowired` on test fields when using `@MockBean` with a slice test. In production application beans — services, repositories, controllers — field injection has no legitimate justification. Even for optional dependencies, `@Autowired(required = false)` on a setter or an `Optional<T>` constructor parameter is cleaner and more explicit.

conceptual.question: What is a circular dependency in Spring? How does Spring detect it and what are the correct ways to fix it?
conceptual.answer:
A **circular dependency** exists when Bean A needs Bean B to be constructed, and Bean B needs Bean A. Neither can be instantiated first. Spring builds a dependency graph and uses topological sort to determine creation order. When it encounters a cycle with constructor injection, it fails immediately at startup: `'The dependencies of some beans in the application context form a cycle'`. This is the correct and safe behavior — you learn about the problem at deploy time, not under production traffic.

There are three fixes, ordered from best to worst:

**Fix 1 — Extract a shared bean.** This is always the right answer. If A and B depend on each other, they share some logic that belongs in a third bean C. A depends on C, B depends on C. No cycle.

**Fix 2 — @Lazy on one injection point.** Spring injects a proxy placeholder into A. B's real instance initializes on first method call. This breaks the cycle but delays validation — wiring errors surface at runtime under traffic rather than at deploy time. Use only as a temporary measure while you plan the proper refactoring.

**Fix 3 — Setter injection for one dependency.** Spring creates both beans first, then calls the setter. The field cannot be `final`. Again, treat as temporary.

followUps.question: Why does constructor injection detect cycles at startup but field injection sometimes does not?
followUps.answer: With **constructor injection**, Spring must have the dependency fully instantiated before it can call the constructor. A cycle is immediately unresolvable — there is no way to create either bean first. With **field injection**, Spring uses a cache of **early singleton references**: it creates Bean A as an incomplete shell (no fields set), puts that shell in the early reference cache, then creates Bean B and injects the A shell into B's field, then goes back and sets A's field to B. The beans are mutually inconsistent during construction but both eventually have all fields set. This silent resolution is dangerous because if Bean A calls Bean B during `@PostConstruct`, and B calls back into A, A's state is incomplete.

followUps.question: You used @Lazy to break a circular dependency and shipped it to production three weeks ago. A senior engineer says it should be removed. What do they mean and how do you approach it?
followUps.answer: @Lazy means the bean initializes on the first method call instead of at startup. The concern is real: startup validation is lost for that bean, meaning any configuration errors surface under live traffic instead of during deployment. The first request to that code path also pays full initialization cost, causing a latency spike. Most importantly, @Lazy signals an unresolved design problem — the cycle still exists. The correct approach is to schedule a refactoring: identify the shared logic that created the cycle, extract it into a third bean, update both original beans to depend on the extracted bean, then remove @Lazy. Verify with tests that cover the previously cyclic code path before deploying.

conceptual.question: What is a BeanDefinition and when is it created relative to bean instances?
conceptual.answer:
A **`BeanDefinition`** is a metadata object that describes how a bean should be created — its class, scope, constructor arguments, factory method, `initMethod`, `destroyMethod`, lazy flag, and dependencies. It is created during Phase 1 of startup, before any bean instance exists. Spring reads your `@Component` annotations, `@Bean` methods, and XML, and converts each declaration into a `BeanDefinition` registered in the `BeanDefinitionRegistry`.

Bean instances are not created until Phase 3. This separation is critical: `BeanFactoryPostProcessors` run between Phase 1 and Phase 3, and they operate on `BeanDefinition` objects — they can modify scope, add properties, or remove definitions entirely. `@Conditional` annotations are evaluated in Phase 2 against the `BeanDefinitionRegistry` — if a condition is false, the definition is removed before any instantiation occurs. Understanding this sequence explains why `@Value("${server.port}")` works — the placeholder is resolved by `PropertySourcesPlaceholderConfigurer` in Phase 2, updating the `BeanDefinition`, before the constructor is called in Phase 3.

followUps.question: Can you modify a BeanDefinition at runtime after the context is started?
followUps.answer: Technically yes through `ConfigurableListableBeanFactory.getBeanDefinition()`, but you should almost never do this in application code. `BeanDefinitionRegistry` modifications after context refresh can cause `BeanDefinition` state to diverge from live singleton instances already created. The legitimate place to modify `BeanDefinitions` is in a `BeanDefinitionRegistryPostProcessor` or `BeanFactoryPostProcessor` during Phase 2, before any bean is instantiated. Spring's auto-configuration system does this — it adds or removes `BeanDefinitions` based on conditions before the instantiation phase begins.

followUps.question: What is the difference between BeanDefinitionRegistryPostProcessor and BeanFactoryPostProcessor?
followUps.answer: `BeanFactoryPostProcessor` runs after all `BeanDefinitions` are loaded and lets you modify existing definitions — for example, changing a property or scope. `BeanDefinitionRegistryPostProcessor` is a sub-interface with an additional method `postProcessBeanDefinitionRegistry()` that runs even earlier and lets you register entirely new `BeanDefinitions`. Spring's `ConfigurationClassPostProcessor` is a `BeanDefinitionRegistryPostProcessor` — it processes `@Configuration` classes and registers all the `@Bean` method definitions. If you need to dynamically register beans based on some external input (like reading a database schema), `BeanDefinitionRegistryPostProcessor` is the correct extension point.

conceptual.question: What is a BeanPostProcessor and how does Spring use it to implement AOP?
conceptual.answer:
A **`BeanPostProcessor`** has two callback methods: `postProcessBeforeInitialization()` runs before `@PostConstruct` and `postProcessAfterInitialization()` runs after. Spring calls these on every single bean in the context after it is instantiated. Your `BeanPostProcessor` can inspect the bean, modify it, or replace it with a completely different object — including a proxy.

This is exactly how AOP annotations work. Spring's `AnnotationAwareAspectJAutoProxyCreator` is a `BeanPostProcessor`. In `postProcessAfterInitialization()`, it checks whether any pointcut matches the bean. If yes, it creates a **CGLIB proxy** (for class-based targets) or **JDK dynamic proxy** (for interface-based targets) that wraps the real bean. The proxy intercepts method calls and applies the advice — starting a transaction, running the async task on a thread pool, checking the cache, etc.

From your code's perspective, `context.getBean(OrderService.class)` returns the proxy, not the real `OrderService`. All external calls go through the proxy and trigger advice. A `this.method()` call inside `OrderService` bypasses the proxy entirely because `this` refers to the real unwrapped object.

followUps.question: What is the difference between CGLIB proxy and JDK dynamic proxy in Spring AOP?
followUps.answer: A **JDK dynamic proxy** can only proxy interfaces — it implements the target's interface and intercepts calls on interface methods. A **CGLIB proxy** subclasses the target class and overrides its methods, so it works even when no interface exists. Spring Boot defaults to CGLIB for `@Transactional` on concrete classes since Spring Boot 2 (the `spring.aop.proxy-target-class=true` default). The practical implication: CGLIB cannot proxy `final` methods or `final` classes — a `@Transactional` method marked `final` will silently not participate in a transaction because CGLIB cannot override it. This is a common gotcha in Spring AOP.

followUps.question: Can you write your own BeanPostProcessor and what would you use it for?
followUps.answer: Yes, implement `BeanPostProcessor` and register the class as a `@Component`. Spring detects `BeanPostProcessor` implementations early and gives them special treatment. Common legitimate uses: logging bean creation timing (measuring how long each `@PostConstruct` takes), injecting custom annotations (scanning for a custom `@Validated` and wiring a validator), or adding monitoring wrappers around service beans. One important rule: a `BeanPostProcessor` bean itself and any beans it depends on are not eligible for AOP proxy wrapping, because the post-processor must be available before the proxy creator can run. This means injecting a `@Transactional` service into your `BeanPostProcessor` will bypass transactions on that service.

conceptual.question: What is the self-invocation proxy bypass problem in Spring and which annotations does it affect?
conceptual.answer:
When a Spring bean calls one of its own methods using `this.method()`, the call bypasses the Spring proxy entirely. Spring's AOP works by wrapping the bean in a proxy object. External callers hit the proxy first, which applies the advice (transaction, async, cache). But `this` refers to the real unwrapped object inside the bean — it has no proxy. So any AOP annotation on an internally called method is completely invisible to Spring at runtime.

Every Spring AOP annotation is affected: **`@Transactional`** (transaction never starts), **`@Async`** (method runs synchronously on the calling thread instead of a thread pool), **`@Cacheable`** (cache is never consulted), **`@Retryable`** (no retry happens), **`@Secured`** and **`@PreAuthorize`** (security check is skipped).

This is one of the most common Spring bugs and it often goes undetected in unit tests because unit tests call the real object directly without a proxy. The bug surfaces in integration tests or production when the annotation's side effect (like a rollback or async execution) is expected but absent.

The fixes: extract the method to a separate `@Component` and call it externally, or inject the bean into itself (`@Autowired private MyService self;`) and call `self.method()`.

followUps.question: How would you write a test that catches a self-invocation proxy bypass bug?
followUps.answer: A unit test calling the real object directly will never catch this because there is no proxy involved. You need an **integration test** that loads a real Spring context and injects the proxied bean. With Spring Boot Test: annotate the test with `@SpringBootTest`, inject the bean with `@Autowired`, and call the outer method that triggers the self-invocation. Then verify the side effect — for `@Transactional` check that a database change was rolled back on exception, for `@Async` check that the method ran on a different thread name. The test fails when self-invocation bypasses the proxy, confirming the bug. Running `mvn -q test` with just unit tests would pass even with the bug present.

followUps.question: Why does injecting the bean into itself (self-injection) work as a fix?
followUps.answer: When you inject `@Autowired private MyService self;`, Spring injects the **proxy** of the service, not `this`. So `self.method()` routes through the proxy, applies all advice, and behaves identically to an external call. This looks odd in code review but is an explicitly supported Spring pattern. The risk is that it can confuse engineers who do not know why `self` exists. A comment explaining the proxy bypass reason is essential. The cleaner long-term fix is to extract the annotated method into a separate `@Component` — this also often improves cohesion because methods that need different transaction or caching behavior frequently belong in different services anyway.

conceptual.question: Explain bean scope mismatch. What is the bug it causes and how do you fix it?
conceptual.answer:
A **scope mismatch** happens when a shorter-lived bean is injected into a longer-lived bean via constructor injection. The most common case: a **prototype**-scoped bean injected into a **singleton**.

Here is why it breaks: constructor injection runs once when the singleton is created. Spring creates one prototype instance, passes it to the constructor, and that single reference is stored in the singleton's `final` field forever. Every subsequent call to the singleton uses the same prototype instance. The prototype's intended shorter lifetime is swallowed by the singleton's longer lifetime.

This is a correctness bug. Prototype scope is typically chosen because the bean holds request-specific or call-specific mutable state that must not be shared between callers. When that state is pinned to a singleton, data leaks between concurrent calls — potentially a security issue.

Three fixes: **(1) `ObjectFactory<T>` injection** — inject `ObjectFactory<RequestParser>` instead of `RequestParser`, call `getObject()` inside each method to get a fresh instance. **(2) `javax.inject.Provider<T>`** — same pattern, portable to non-Spring contexts. **(3) `@Lookup` method injection** — Spring overrides the annotated method via CGLIB to return a new prototype each time; works but couples the class to CGLIB.

followUps.question: What is the difference between prototype scope and request scope for per-call state in a web application?
followUps.answer: **Prototype scope** creates a new instance every time `getBean()` is called, with no lifecycle tied to HTTP. You control the lifecycle yourself via `ObjectFactory`. **Request scope** creates one instance per HTTP request and destroys it when the request completes — the lifecycle is managed by Spring's `RequestContextHolder`. For web services, request scope is almost always the better choice for per-request state (user context, trace IDs, request-level caches) because the lifecycle boundary is clear and automatic. Prototype scope is better for non-web use cases — fresh parsers, validators, or value objects — where you need explicit per-call construction.

followUps.question: A @Scheduled background job uses a @RequestScope bean and fails with ScopeNotActiveException. Why?
followUps.answer: Scheduled methods run on a background thread managed by the Spring task executor. That thread has no active HTTP request context — `RequestContextHolder` finds nothing when the request-scoped bean tries to look up its backing store. Spring throws `ScopeNotActiveException`. The root cause is a design error: the scheduler is trying to operate on request-scoped state, which only makes sense within the lifecycle of an HTTP request. The fix is to refactor the scheduled task to fetch the data it needs from a repository, cache, or event store rather than depending on a request-scoped bean. Never inject request-scoped beans into singleton schedulers or background workers.

conceptual.question: What is @Configuration and why does Spring proxy it with CGLIB?

followUps.question: When would you use @Configuration(proxyBeanMethods = false)?
followUps.answer: `proxyBeanMethods = false` skips CGLIB subclassing and makes the configuration class a plain Java class. This improves startup time (no CGLIB processing) and works in GraalVM native images where runtime class subclassing is restricted. Use it when your `@Bean` methods do not call each other internally — typically in library auto-configuration classes where each bean is independent. Spring Boot's own auto-configurations almost all use `proxyBeanMethods = false` for this reason. If your `@Configuration` class has `@Bean` methods that reference each other, you must keep the default (proxy mode) to preserve singleton semantics.

followUps.question: What is the difference between @Component and @Configuration when both contain @Bean methods?
followUps.answer: A `@Component` class with `@Bean` methods is called a **lite mode** configuration. CGLIB does not proxy it. `@Bean` methods in a `@Component` class behave like factory methods — calling one from another creates a new instance every time. This is intentional for `@Component` because it can be a regular bean doing work, with some factory methods as a convenience. `@Configuration` is specifically designed for wiring, with full proxy behavior to enforce singleton semantics between inter-dependent `@Bean` methods. The safe rule: if your configuration class has `@Bean` methods that reference each other, use `@Configuration`. If the `@Bean` methods are independent or the class does other work, `@Component` lite mode is acceptable but must be understood.

conceptual.question: How does Spring decide which bean to inject when multiple beans implement the same interface?
conceptual.answer:
Spring has a resolution chain for **ambiguous bean injection**.

**Step 1 — Type match.** Spring finds all beans that match the injection point's type. If exactly one, it injects that one.

**Step 2 — @Primary.** If multiple beans match, Spring picks the one annotated with `@Primary`. Only one bean per type should be `@Primary`.

**Step 3 — @Qualifier.** If neither of the above resolves it, Spring looks for `@Qualifier("beanName")` at the injection point and matches by bean name. `@Qualifier` on a constructor parameter or field is the explicit disambiguation.

**Step 4 — Injection point name.** If no `@Qualifier` is present, Spring tries to match the field name or constructor parameter name to a bean name. This is fragile — parameter names can be erased by the compiler unless you use `-parameters`.

**Step 5 — NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException.** If none of the above resolves the ambiguity, startup fails.

For plugin architectures, inject `List<PaymentProcessor>` to get all implementations, or `Map<String, PaymentProcessor>` to get them keyed by bean name for runtime selection.

followUps.question: What is the difference between @Primary and @ConditionalOnMissingBean?
followUps.answer: `@Primary` is a runtime hint — it tells Spring which bean to prefer when there are multiple candidates at the injection point. It always takes effect regardless of what other beans exist. `@ConditionalOnMissingBean` is a startup-time condition — the annotated bean is only registered if no bean of that type already exists. In library code (Spring starters), `@ConditionalOnMissingBean` is the correct pattern because it lets application code override the library's default. `@Primary` in a library would silently win over application beans, which is a violation of the override contract. The rule: `@Primary` for application code where you control all beans, `@ConditionalOnMissingBean` for library code where the consumer should be able to override.

followUps.question: How would you select between multiple PaymentProcessor implementations at runtime based on the payment method in the request?
followUps.answer: Inject `Map<String, PaymentProcessor> processors` — Spring automatically wires all `PaymentProcessor` beans into the map, keyed by bean name. At runtime, look up by key: `processors.get(paymentMethod)`. For more control, have each `PaymentProcessor` expose a `supports(String method)` method, inject `List<PaymentProcessor>`, and filter at runtime: `processors.stream().filter(p -> p.supports(method)).findFirst()`. The map approach is simpler when bean names match payment method strings. The filter approach is cleaner when the selection logic is more complex. Both avoid `if-else` chains and satisfy the Open/Closed Principle — adding a new payment method means adding a new `@Component` with no changes to the routing logic.

conceptual.question: What is the difference between BeanFactory and ApplicationContext? Why does Spring Boot always use ApplicationContext?
conceptual.answer:
`BeanFactory` is the root IoC container interface — it can create and wire beans lazily on demand via `getBean()`. It is the minimal abstraction.

`ApplicationContext` extends `BeanFactory` and adds six capabilities that production applications need:

1. **Eager singleton initialization** — all singletons are created at startup, so wiring errors fail fast on deploy rather than on first request under traffic.
2. **Event publishing** via `ApplicationEventPublisher` — beans can publish and listen to events without direct coupling.
3. **Internationalization** via `MessageSource` — resolves locale-specific message codes.
4. **Resource abstraction** via `ResourceLoader` — unified access to classpath, filesystem, and URL resources.
5. **Environment abstraction** — profiles and property sources for configuration.
6. **BeanPostProcessor auto-detection** — automatically discovers and applies BeanPostProcessors.

Spring Boot's `SpringApplication.run()` creates an `AnnotationConfigServletWebServerApplicationContext` for web apps. Raw `BeanFactory` is only useful inside the framework itself. Using it in application code bypasses all six capabilities listed above.

followUps.question: Why is calling ApplicationContext.getBean() in application code considered an anti-pattern?
followUps.answer: Calling `context.getBean(OrderService.class)` throughout application code is the **Service Locator pattern** — the class reaches into the container to pull out its dependencies instead of declaring them. This hides dependencies (you cannot tell what a class needs by looking at its constructor), makes testing impossible without a live context (you cannot inject mocks), and creates implicit coupling to the container. It defeats the entire purpose of DI. The correct approach is always to declare dependencies in the constructor and let the container inject them. The only legitimate use of `getBean()` in application code is in a small number of integration points — a custom `ApplicationRunner` that bootstraps the app, or a legacy bridge between Spring and non-Spring code.

followUps.question: What is an ApplicationEventPublisher and how does it relate to IoC?
followUps.answer: `ApplicationEventPublisher` lets a bean publish an event — `publisher.publishEvent(new OrderCreatedEvent(order))` — without knowing who handles it. Listeners are separate `@Component` beans with `@EventListener` methods. The container delivers the event to all matching listeners. This is IoC applied to communication: the publishing bean does not call the listener directly; the container calls the listener on the publisher's behalf. This decoupling is useful for cross-cutting concerns like audit logging, cache eviction, or notification sending that should happen after a domain event but should not be the domain service's direct responsibility. In a transactional context, `@TransactionalEventListener` ensures the listener runs only after the transaction commits.

conceptual.question: What does @Lazy do in Spring and when is it appropriate to use it?
conceptual.answer:
By default, Spring instantiates all singleton beans at startup (**eager initialization**). `@Lazy` defers bean instantiation to the first time the bean is requested — either via `getBean()` or when an `@Autowired` injection point is first accessed. Spring injects a lightweight CGLIB proxy placeholder instead of the real bean. When the proxy is first invoked, it initializes the real bean and delegates the call.

When `@Lazy` is appropriate:
- **Breaking a circular dependency temporarily** while you plan a proper refactoring.
- **Beans with expensive initialization** that are not always needed — e.g., a reporting service only used during scheduled jobs.
- **Testing** where you want to start a context without a subset of slow beans.

When `@Lazy` is harmful:
- **Startup validation is bypassed** — wiring errors in a lazily initialized bean surface at runtime under traffic, not at deploy time.
- **First-request latency spike** — the first call pays full initialization cost.
- **`spring.main.lazy-initialization=true`** defers all beans, which may cut startup time but means your first requests are slow and wiring errors are hidden.

The strong default is eager initialization — fail fast, fail at deploy, not under traffic.

followUps.question: You have a Spring Boot service with 600 beans and startup takes 45 seconds. How would you reduce it?
followUps.answer: First, **measure before optimizing**. Enable `management.endpoint.startup.enabled=true` and hit `/actuator/startup` after boot — it shows every bean initialization with duration. Anything over 100ms is a candidate. Add `--debug` to find unnecessary auto-configurations activating. Common fixes: narrow `@SpringBootApplication(scanBasePackages)` to exclude packages with no beans, convert slow `@PostConstruct` network calls to `ApplicationRunner` background tasks, use `@ConditionalOnProperty` to disable unused auto-configurations. For a structural fix, upgrade to Spring Boot 3 and enable **Spring AOT** (`./mvnw spring-boot:build-image` with GraalVM native profile) — AOT pre-computes BeanDefinitions at build time, eliminating runtime classpath scanning. Native image startup can drop from 45 seconds to under 2 seconds.

followUps.question: If Spring Boot defaults to eager initialization for correctness, why does lazy initialization exist at all?
followUps.answer: Lazy initialization exists for specific legitimate trade-offs. In **serverless and FaaS environments** (AWS Lambda, Cloud Run), the container is created per request and startup time directly affects latency and billing — eager init of 400 beans is unacceptable. In **large monoliths with bounded contexts**, some subsystems may only be needed for specific request paths — lazy init avoids loading them for unrelated requests. In **integration testing**, loading only the beans under test with `@Import` or lazy init for the rest makes test suites faster. The key is that you are consciously accepting the trade-off between startup speed and validation coverage. Spring Boot's philosophy is correct for production services: fail fast. Lazy init is a situational optimization, not a default.

conceptual.question: How do you inject all beans of a given type into a list in Spring, and what is this pattern used for?
conceptual.answer:
Declaring `List<PaymentProcessor> processors` as a constructor parameter (or `@Autowired` field) tells Spring to inject all beans in the context that implement `PaymentProcessor`. Every `@Component` or `@Bean` method returning a `PaymentProcessor` implementation is included automatically.

This is the **plugin pattern**: the core routing class depends on the interface, not on any specific implementation. Adding a new implementation means adding a new `@Component` — zero changes to the router. This satisfies the **Open/Closed Principle**: open for extension (add bean), closed for modification (router unchanged).

**Ordering**: implement `Ordered` or annotate with `@Order(1)` to control injection order.

**Map injection**: `Map<String, PaymentProcessor>` injects all implementations keyed by bean name — useful for runtime selection by name.

**Provider/ObjectFactory**: `List<ObjectFactory<PaymentProcessor>>` injects factories if each call needs a fresh instance.

Real-world uses: multi-channel notification systems, payment gateway routing, feature flag evaluators, validation chains, health indicator aggregators.

followUps.question: What happens if no beans of the injected list type exist at all?
followUps.answer: Spring injects an **empty list** rather than throwing. This is by design — a `List<T>` injection point is implicitly optional. If you want to require at least one implementation, you can check the list in `@PostConstruct` and throw a configuration exception. If you are using `@Autowired` on a list field, adding `@Autowired(required = true)` still injects an empty list when no beans match (unlike single-bean injection which throws). The idiomatic Spring way to enforce at least one implementation is a validator in `@PostConstruct`: `if (processors.isEmpty()) throw new IllegalStateException("No PaymentProcessor beans registered");`.

followUps.question: How would a third-party JAR add a new PaymentProcessor to your application without any code change in your service?
followUps.answer: The third-party JAR uses Spring Boot's **auto-configuration** mechanism. The JAR includes a `@Configuration` class that defines a `@Bean` returning their `PaymentProcessor` implementation. That configuration class is listed in `META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports` (Boot 2.7+). When the JAR is on the classpath, Spring Boot reads that file during Phase 1 and processes the auto-configuration, registering the new `PaymentProcessor` bean. Your service's `List<PaymentProcessor>` injection point automatically includes the new implementation. This is exactly how all Spring Boot starters work — they self-register without any import statement in your application code.

conceptual.question: What is @Component scanning and how does it work internally? What are its performance trade-offs?
conceptual.answer:
**Component scanning** is triggered by `@SpringBootApplication` (which includes `@ComponentScan`). Spring uses `ClassPathScanningCandidateComponentProvider` to scan the specified packages. For each class file on the classpath within those packages, it reads the bytecode metadata (without loading the class) and checks for qualifying annotations — `@Component`, `@Service`, `@Repository`, `@Controller`, `@RestController`, or any meta-annotation built on `@Component`. Qualifying classes become `BeanDefinition` objects.

The process is **I/O and CPU intensive**: a large classpath with 5,000+ class files takes measurable time. Spring uses bytecode reading (via ASM) rather than classloading to keep it fast, but the scan still has a cost proportional to the number of classes in the scan path.

Performance trade-offs:
- Wide `scanBasePackages` (`com.mycompany` with 3,000 classes) → slow startup
- Narrow packages (`com.mycompany.core.service`) → faster
- Explicit `@Bean` definitions in `@Configuration` → fastest (no scan needed for those beans)
- Spring Boot 3 with AOT → scan results computed at build time, not runtime

followUps.question: What is the double-scan bug where beans are registered twice?
followUps.answer: The classic trap: `@SpringBootApplication` is on `com.myapp` which scans `com.myapp.**`, and somewhere in that package is a `@Configuration` class that also has `@ComponentScan("com.myapp")`. Spring executes both scans and registers each `BeanDefinition` twice. In Spring Boot 2.1+, `spring.main.allow-bean-definition-overriding` defaults to `false`, so the second registration throws `BeanDefinitionOverrideException`. In earlier versions, the second definition silently overwrites the first. The fix: remove the redundant `@ComponentScan`. One `@SpringBootApplication` at the root owns scanning. Always grep for `@ComponentScan` in a codebase you inherit — multiple scan declarations are almost always a mistake.

followUps.question: When would you choose explicit @Bean methods over component scanning?
followUps.answer: Use explicit `@Bean` methods for **third-party types** you do not own (you cannot add `@Component` to a Hikari `DataSource` or a Kafka `ProducerFactory`), for **infrastructure beans** where construction order and parameters matter (connection pools, topic templates, security filters), and for **conditional bean registration** based on environment or properties. Use component scanning for your own application classes — services, repositories, controllers — where the annotation is self-documenting and consistent. The team rule should be: scanning for domain classes, explicit `@Bean` for integration points. Never mix both patterns for the same type without a clear decision on which owns it.

conceptual.question: What is the Spring Environment abstraction and how does it relate to bean configuration?
conceptual.answer:
The **`Environment`** abstraction unifies two things: **profiles** (which `@Configuration` classes or beans are active) and **property sources** (where configuration values come from).

A **profile** is a named group of beans. `@Profile("prod")` on a `@Bean` or `@Configuration` means that bean is only registered when the `prod` profile is active. `spring.profiles.active=prod` activates it. This lets you swap entire bean graphs — a real database pool in prod, an in-memory H2 in tests — without `if` statements in your code.

A **property source** is a location from which `${...}` placeholders are resolved. Spring Boot's `PropertySourcesPlaceholderConfigurer` searches in priority order: command-line args → system properties → environment variables → `application.properties`. `@Value("${server.port:8080}")` resolves `server.port` from this chain, defaulting to `8080`.

Profiles and properties together make the same code deployable to dev, staging, and production without source changes — only externalized configuration changes.

followUps.question: What is @ConfigurationProperties and when is it better than @Value?
followUps.answer: `@ConfigurationProperties(prefix = "payment")` binds all properties under a prefix to a Java class's fields. `@Value` binds one property to one field. For a single scalar like `server.port`, `@Value` is simpler. For a group of related properties (`payment.gateway.url`, `payment.gateway.timeout`, `payment.retry.maxAttempts`), `@ConfigurationProperties` is much better: the binding class is a single place to look, types are validated at startup, IDE completion works, and the class can be unit-tested by setting fields directly without a Spring context. Annotate the binding class with `@Validated` and use `@NotNull` to fail fast if required properties are missing.

followUps.question: How do you inject different implementations of an interface in different Spring profiles?

conceptual.question: What is the difference between @Value and @ConfigurationProperties? When do you use each?
conceptual.answer:
**`@Value`** binds a single property to a single field: `@Value("${server.port:8080}") int port`. It is evaluated by `PropertySourcesPlaceholderConfigurer` during Phase 2 of startup. It works anywhere a Spring bean is created — in `@Service`, `@Component`, or `@Bean` method parameters. The field can be `final` when used in a constructor parameter.

**`@ConfigurationProperties`** binds all properties under a prefix to a class: `@ConfigurationProperties(prefix = "payment")`. All fields under `payment.*` are bound automatically by type-safe relaxed binding — `payment.gateway-url` maps to `gatewayUrl`. Add `@Validated` on the class to fail fast at startup if required properties are missing.

**Rule of thumb**: use `@Value` for a single scalar (a port, a feature flag, a timeout). Use `@ConfigurationProperties` when you have 3+ related properties — they form a cohesive configuration group that belongs in one class, can be documented together, supports IDE completion, and can be unit tested without a Spring context by setting fields directly.

followUps.question: How do you enforce that a @ConfigurationProperties field is required and not null?
followUps.answer: Add `@Validated` on the `@ConfigurationProperties` class and annotate required fields with `@NotNull` (or `@NotBlank` for strings). Spring Boot's binder validates the bound properties at startup using Hibernate Validator. If a required property is missing, startup fails with `BindValidationException` listing every missing field by name. This is much better than `@Value` with no default, which fails one property at a time with a generic `Could not resolve placeholder` message. With `@ConfigurationProperties` and `@Validated`, you get all missing properties in one error and a clear message telling you the expected prefix.

followUps.question: Can you use @ConfigurationProperties with records in modern Java?
followUps.answer: Yes, since Spring Boot 2.6. Annotate the record with `@ConfigurationProperties(prefix = "...")` and declare a canonical constructor — Spring Boot uses constructor binding automatically for records. All components are bound from the matching property names. Add `@Validated` on the record and JSR-303 annotations on the components to enforce constraints. The record approach is the cleanest because the fields are automatically `final` (immutable configuration), the canonical constructor serves as the binding entry point, and there are no setters to worry about. Register with `@EnableConfigurationProperties(MyConfigRecord.class)` or use `@ConfigurationPropertiesScan`.

conceptual.question: What is FactoryBean in Spring and how does Spring Data use it to create repository proxies?
conceptual.answer:
**FactoryBean<T>** is a Spring SPI interface that allows a registered bean to act as a factory producing a different object. When a class implementing FactoryBean<UserRepository> is registered, Spring calls getObject() at startup and registers the *product* in the context — not the factory itself. Calling context.getBean("userRepo") returns the UserRepository product. Calling context.getBean("&userRepo") (the & prefix) returns the FactoryBean instance.

**Spring Data uses FactoryBean for every repository interface.** When @EnableJpaRepositories is processed, a JpaRepositoriesRegistrar (ImportBeanDefinitionRegistrar) scans for interfaces extending Repository<T, ID> and registers a JpaRepositoryFactoryBean BeanDefinition for each one. At startup, JpaRepositoryFactoryBean.getObject() creates a JDK dynamic proxy implementing your interface, backed by Spring Data's query resolution engine.

This is why you can @Autowired UserRepository even though you only declared an interface — FactoryBean is the bridge between interface declaration and a runtime proxy. The FactoryBean pattern is also used by MyBatis SqlSessionFactoryBean, Hibernate LocalSessionFactoryBean, and any library that needs complex multi-step construction of a bean.

followUps.question: How would you detect in a running context that a particular bean was created by a FactoryBean?
followUps.answer: Three approaches: (1) context.isFactoryBean("beanName") returns true if the backing BeanDefinition comes from a FactoryBean. (2) In the context's bean names list, the name appears once for the product — call context.getBean("&beanName") to retrieve the factory itself; if that succeeds and returns a FactoryBean, you know. (3) In the BeanDefinition registry, the registered class is the FactoryBean implementation class, not the product type — context.getBeanDefinition("beanName").getBeanClassName() returns JpaRepositoryFactoryBean, not UserRepository. In practical debugging, the Spring startup log with --debug shows each bean's class name, which reveals FactoryBeans.

followUps.question: Can you write your own FactoryBean and when would you use it over a plain @Bean method?
followUps.answer: Yes — implement FactoryBean<T> on a @Component or @Bean-declared class. getObject() builds the product, getObjectType() returns the type for autowiring, isSingleton() controls caching. Prefer FactoryBean over @Bean when: (1) the factory itself needs multiple injected Spring beans as constructor parameters, and passing them all to a @Bean method would be unwieldy; (2) isSingleton() needs to return false to create a new product per injection point; (3) you are writing reusable library code where consumers configure the factory bean in their context rather than calling your @Bean method. For application code, a @Bean method is almost always cleaner and more readable — reserve FactoryBean for integrations and frameworks.

conceptual.question: How does Spring's three-level singleton cache work, and why did Spring Boot 2.6 disable circular reference resolution by default?
conceptual.answer:
**DefaultSingletonBeanRegistry** manages singleton creation with three caches: singletonFactories (ObjectFactory lambdas placed immediately after constructor call), earlySingletonObjects (early bean references after first factory invocation), and singletonObjects (fully initialized singletons).
